summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that an unauthenticated attacker can write arbitrary files to the server's filesystem via the output_path parameter in the POST /screenshot endpoint. The exploit writes a screenshot file to an arbitrary path (/tmp/evohunt_arbitrary_write_proof.png), demonstrating unrestricted file write.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should cause the crawl4ai server to write a screenshot file to an arbitrary attacker-specified path (/tmp/evohunt_arbitrary_write_proof.png), demonstrating that the output_path parameter has no path restriction.
actual_observed_result: The POST /screenshot endpoint wrote a PNG file to the specified arbitrary path. The file was created with non-zero size, confirming that the output_path parameter is used without any path validation or restriction.
verification_method: The verify.sh script checks for the existence and non-zero size of the file at the arbitrary output_path location after sending the exploit request. Before the exploit, the script ensures the file does not exist. After the exploit, the script confirms the file was created with non-zero content. This distinguishes a real arbitrary file write from a generic API error or startup failure.
evidence_produced_by_run: A file was created at /tmp/evohunt_arbitrary_write_proof.png with non-zero size.
No authentication token was required to exploit the /screenshot endpoint.
The output_path parameter was passed through os.path.abspath() and open() without any directory restriction.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable tag, and uses it to build the base crawl4ai Docker image. A second build step layers the exploit scripts on top.
